text: The surgical team uses the assistant port to place wristed robotic instruments. The pelvis is surveyed with the patient in Trendelenburg position, and pneumoperitoneum is established. The sacral promontory is identified, and the ureter is visualized.
The surgeon retrograde instills the bladder through the Foley catheter, visibly delineating the bladder's borders.

The surgeon incises the peritoneum overlying the cervix using monopolar shears, revealing the pubic cervical fascia as the peritoneum is dissected away. Adhesiolysis is performed anteriorly to the cervix using sharp dissection and electrosurgical energy, with a medium malleable retractor aiding in the vaginal dissection. Posteriorly, the peritoneum is tented up and dissected off the cervix, again exposing the pubic cervical fascia, with malleable retractors assisting in the dissection.

The completed anterior and posterior dissections are visible prior to mesh placement. The polypropylene Y mesh is prepared extracorporeally, with one arm sutured in an accordion fashion for easier intra-abdominal management. The mesh is placed over the cervical stump, and its apex is secured to the cervix with permanent interrupted sutures.
The mesh is secured with the superior arm placed under traction. The surgeon identifies the sacral promontory and incises the overlying peritoneum, revealing underlying structures.

The surgeon extends the peritoneal incision medially toward the posterior vaginal incision to avoid ureteric injury. The thin anterior longitudinal ligament overlying the sacral promontory is dissected, along with the middle sacral vessels. The vessels are desiccated using fenestrated bipolar forceps to prevent hemorrhage. The mesh is then secured to the anterior longitudinal ligament at the hollow of the sacrum with permanent interrupted sutures.

The surgeon demonstrates the final placement of the mesh, extending from the cervix to the sacral promontory. The mesh is re-peritonealized using a 2-0 V-lock suture to prevent a clothesline effect on the bowel, ensuring adequate apical support.
